sentence: I like the fact I've worn different hats.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 여러 역할을 두루 맡다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use it when one person covers several roles or job titles — a staple of early-stage startup introductions.

context: Reflecting on his career, Bret says his strength comes from having moved through wildly different roles — engineer, founder, CTO, and co-CEO.

sentence: It's so hard to sell productivity software, which I learned the hard way.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 호되게 직접 겪으며 깨닫다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use it to admit you gained a lesson through failure or trial and error — it sounds humble and genuine.

context: Bret is talking about Quip, the collaborative-docs startup he founded. Even with a great product, he discovered as a founder just how hard productivity software is to sell.

vocabulary.meaning: software for documents, collaboration, and getting work done

sentence: Just putting a database in the cloud was hard and that alone was a technical moat.

keyPhrase.usageTip: Use it for a defensible advantage competitors can't easily copy. "What's your moat?" is a staple investor question.

context: Recalling the early cloud era, Bret explains that even now-obvious technology was so hard to build back then that engineering skill alone created a competitive advantage.

vocabulary.meaning: a castle's defensive ditch; figuratively, a barrier competitors can't cross

sentence: It honestly wasn't the most important part of Google Maps, but it was the sizzle to the steak.

sentence: They interacted with me as, for lack of a better word, a suit, the boss.

sentence: I went home that night and I was stewing on it and not very happy.

sentence: What was my plan to manage them out of the company?

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: (직원을) 완곡하게 내보내다, 퇴출시키다
keyPhrase.usageTip: A softer HR phrase than "fire" — moving someone out through the performance-management process.

context: On handling underperformers, Bret's point is that a leader needs a concrete plan for a struggling team member — not vague waiting.

sentence: You're probably going to bring to bear a lot of things.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: (역량·자원을) 총동원해 활용하다
keyPhrase.usageTip: A formal expression for deploying your resources — experience, skills, network — against a problem.

context: Advice for starting something new: your accumulated experience and skills all get mobilized when you face a new problem.

vocabulary.meaning: here: to bring force or resources into action
vocabulary.note: Easily confused with "bare" — same pronunciation

sentence: Yeah, try out Sierra. I'm a single issue voter.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 딱 한 가지만 보고 미는 사람 (여기선 'Sierra만 민다'는 농담)
keyPhrase.usageTip: Originally a politics term for voters who decide on one issue alone; repurposed as a joke meaning "I only care about this one thing."

context: At the episode's plug time, asked what he wants listeners to remember, Bret jokes he'll only push one thing — his company Sierra — twisting a political term.

sentence: And so I thought, all right, I'm going to toss my hat in the ring and not overthink it.

keyPhrase.text: toss one's hat in the ring
keyPhrase.surface: toss my hat in the ring
keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 도전장을 내밀다. 겁먹지 않고 일단 뛰어들다

context: Wes Kao is describing her decision to pursue an opportunity she was uncertain about. Rather than letting self-doubt paralyze her, she chose to simply put herself forward and stop overanalyzing whether she was ready.

vocabulary.note: Common in business contexts where analysis paralysis is a risk

sentence: But my lesson there is don't take yourself out of the running before you get rejected.

keyPhrase.surface: take yourself out of the running
keyPhrase.meaning_ko: (거절당하기도 전에) 스스로 경쟁에서 발을 빼다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this as a coaching reminder when someone is hesitating to apply for a role or pitch an idea because they assume they'll be rejected.

context: Wes Kao is sharing a career lesson about self-sabotage: many people disqualify themselves mentally and stop applying for roles or opportunities before anyone has actually turned them down, which guarantees failure.

vocabulary.meaning: formally turned down or not selected by someone else

sentence: I think externally, he can sometimes be a little bit of a vague Buddha, if you will.

keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this phrase (with tact) to describe a leader or communicator whose public statements feel lofty and inspirational but lack specificity that others can act on.

vocabulary.meaning: not clearly expressed or defined; intentionally or unintentionally ambiguous

keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this when coaching someone on a presentation or written proposal that buries the main point under too much setup — tell them to cut the backstory scope creep.

sentence: With Twitter at least, it's 280 characters. It's something short that you're saying that's a little bit of a mic drop.

keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this to describe the ideal ending of a pitch, presentation, or social post — a single line that lands so well it needs no follow-up.

keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this when offering to handle a colleague's or manager's responsibility — it signals empathy and initiative in professional settings.

context: Wes Kao is describing what high-performing employees do proactively: they identify the problems that are stressing out their managers and make it their mission to solve them, removing the burden from their boss's to-do list.

vocabulary.meaning: causing anxiety or concern; here used as a participle meaning 'what was causing stress'

sentence: They're great at keeping their bosses in the loop on what's happening so their bosses, aren't constantly having to ask and pepper them with questions every day

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: (상황 변화를) 빠짐없이 공유해 상대가 흐름에서 끊기지 않게 하다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this phrase when explaining why proactive communication matters — keeping stakeholders in the loop reduces unnecessary meetings and micromanagement.

context: Wes Kao is explaining a habit of effective employees: by proactively sharing status updates with their managers, they prevent a dynamic where the manager must constantly chase them for information.

vocabulary.meaning: to direct a rapid series of questions or requests at someone

sentence: I find especially in remote work too erring on the side of over communicating is just, it ends up being the right level of communication.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 불확실할 때 더 안전한 쪽으로 기울다

context: Wes Kao is giving advice about remote-work communication norms. When you're unsure how much to share with teammates or managers, defaulting to more communication consistently turns out to be the right choice.

vocabulary.meaning: making a slight mistake or deviation; here used idiomatically to mean 'choosing to lean toward'
vocabulary.note: From the verb 'to err'; in this idiom it means a deliberate, safe choice, not a mistake

sentence: Yeah. Saying no does not come naturally for me either as a recovering people pleaser.

keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this self-deprecating phrase when acknowledging that saying no is difficult for you — it shows self-awareness and signals you're working on boundaries.

context: Wes Kao is being candid about a personal growth challenge: her default is to say yes to avoid conflict, and she frames herself as 'recovering' — as if from a habit — to show she's actively working against the pattern.

vocabulary.meaning: without effort or resistance; as an instinctive default response
vocabulary.note: 'Does not come naturally' means the behavior requires conscious effort, not instinct

sentence: I would ask him like, "Hey, can you design this for me? Can you design that? And, oh, here's another thing I'm going to throw over the wall to you."

keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this as a warning against lazy handoffs — when work is thrown over the wall, the recipient lacks context, causing rework and friction between teams.

context: Wes Kao is sharing a past mistake she made when working with a designer: she repeatedly sent ad-hoc design requests without proper briefs or context, treating the collaboration like a one-way handoff rather than a partnership.

vocabulary.meaning: here used as a verb: to create a visual or product artifact according to functional and aesthetic requirements
vocabulary.note: Context-dependent — 'design this' means produce a deliverable, not just think about it

sentence: Because what everyone really wants is clarity. And what everyone really wants is to be able to row in the same direction really quickly.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 팀 전체가 같은 목표를 향해 일사불란하게 움직이다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this phrase when emphasizing team alignment — especially in all-hands meetings, strategy decks, or offsites where you need everyone pulling toward a single goal.

context: Brian Chesky is explaining why clear leadership and company-wide alignment matter. He argues that when people understand the direction, they can coordinate their efforts and move together without friction.

vocabulary.meaning: the quality of being clear and easy to understand; freedom from ambiguity
vocabulary.note: In leadership contexts, 'clarity' often refers to a well-communicated strategy or direction.

sentence: And there's this negative term called micromanagement. I think there's a difference between micromanagement, which is like telling people exactly what to do, and being in the details.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 세부 사항을 직접 파악하고 현장에 밀착해 관여하다. 마이크로매니지먼트(통제)와는 다른 주도적 디테일 감각
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this phrase to describe a hands-on, thorough leadership style that should not be confused with controlling or undermining your team.

context: Brian Chesky is distinguishing between two leadership styles: micromanagement, which he views as counterproductive, and a detail-oriented approach that keeps leaders informed and effective. He argues that knowing the details is a sign of good leadership, not a character flaw.

vocabulary.meaning: a management style where a leader closely controls every aspect of their team's work, leaving little autonomy
vocabulary.note: Often used negatively; Chesky contrasts it with the more constructive 'being in the details.'

sentence: We are a fairly purely functional model. They manage by influence. They do not have control.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 공식 권한이 아닌 신뢰와 설득력으로 사람을 움직여 성과를 이끌다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this phrase when describing how leaders in flat or matrix organizations get things done without formal reporting lines — common in tech companies and cross-functional teams.

context: Brian Chesky is describing Airbnb's functional organizational model, where leaders such as department heads do not have command-and-control authority over cross-functional teams. Instead, they achieve outcomes by earning trust and persuading others.

vocabulary.meaning: an organizational structure where employees are grouped by their specialty or function (e.g., engineering, design, marketing) rather than by product or business unit
vocabulary.note: Airbnb's 'fairly purely functional model' means each function is centralized under one leader rather than embedded in separate product teams.

sentence: And then the pandemic occurred and we lost 80% of our business in eight weeks. And then suddenly we're like, oh my God. I remember basically staring into the abyss.

keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this vivid idiom when describing a pivotal, worst-case-scenario moment in a company or career — it signals honest vulnerability and is especially powerful in founder or investor storytelling.

context: Brian Chesky is recounting the early months of the COVID-19 pandemic, when travel came to a near-complete halt and Airbnb's revenue collapsed by 80% in just eight weeks. He uses this phrase to capture the existential terror he felt as the company's survival became uncertain.

vocabulary.meaning: a deep, seemingly bottomless void; used figuratively to describe a crisis with no visible end or solution
vocabulary.note: Derived from Greek 'abyssos' (bottomless). In business contexts, it signals existential threat.

sentence: And that's what happened to our business. We had a near-death business experience and our business flashed before our eyes.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 회사가 무너지기 직전까지 몰린 사활의 위기. 인생의 임사체험을 비즈니스에 빗댄 표현
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use 'near-death experience' (or 'near-death business experience') when recounting a company-threatening crisis that ultimately forced a major strategic pivot — a staple phrase in founder talks and post-mortems.

context: Brian Chesky is describing how the COVID-19 pandemic pushed Airbnb to the brink of collapse, with revenue evaporating almost overnight. He explains that this brush with failure became the turning point that made him completely rethink how he ran the company.

vocabulary.meaning: a rapid mental replay of key memories or events, commonly described by people who believe they are about to die; here used metaphorically for the company's history
vocabulary.note: Chesky borrows from the idiom 'my life flashed before my eyes' to emphasize how close Airbnb came to total failure.

sentence: As you know, there was a saying inside of Airbnb, it was add a zero. Add a zero at the end, which is to imagine something you order, imagine it bigger.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 목표 숫자 끝에 0을 하나 더 붙이듯 야망의 스케일을 10배로 키우다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this phrase in brainstorming, product strategy, or fundraising conversations to push a team beyond linear thinking and toward 10x bets — particularly effective when challenging assumptions about market size or product ambition.

context: Brian Chesky is sharing one of Airbnb's internal mantras for ambitious thinking. The phrase means that whatever scale or quantity you are imagining, you should tack a zero onto it — 10 becomes 100, 1,000 becomes 10,000 — forcing you to reimagine scope entirely rather than just optimizing what already exists.

sentence: And fast decisions come from a bias of action. If we're in a meeting, we don't just say, "Okay, let's circle back on this next week."

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 완벽한 정보를 기다리기보다 지금 당장 결정하고 움직이는 것을 기본값으로 삼다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this phrase to describe (or advocate for) a culture that favors making imperfect decisions quickly over waiting for perfect information — widely used in startup culture and performance reviews.

context: Brian Chesky is explaining how Airbnb maintains speed in its decision-making culture. He contrasts a bias for action with the common corporate habit of deferring decisions — the reflexive 'let's circle back' — arguing that decisive action in meetings is a competitive advantage.

vocabulary.meaning: to return to a topic or decision at a later time, often used to defer rather than resolve
vocabulary.note: A common corporate filler phrase; Chesky uses it here as an example of what a bias-for-action culture actively avoids.

sentence: And we might call this first principle thinking. What are the foundational elements that comprise this problem and how can we reconstruct them?

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 기존 통념이나 전례를 배제하고 문제를 가장 근본적인 사실로 쪼갠 뒤 처음부터 해법을 구축하다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this phrase in strategy or product conversations when proposing to abandon assumptions and rethink a problem from scratch — it signals rigorous, original thinking and is a hallmark phrase in Silicon Valley discourse.

context: Brian Chesky is describing the analytical method Airbnb uses to tackle complex strategic problems. Rather than copying how others have solved similar challenges, first principle thinking means identifying the irreducible building blocks of a problem and constructing a novel answer from those fundamentals.

vocabulary.meaning: relating to the most basic, underlying principles or components upon which everything else is built
to build something again from its basic parts, often after analyzing what those parts are
vocabulary.note: Here Chesky uses 'reconstruct' to mean designing a fresh solution from first principles rather than patching an existing one.

sentence: And you pay it forward. I think that it's just one continuation of the valley of people helping one another, learning from one another. And I also feel like I learned by teaching as well.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 받은 도움을 베풀어준 사람에게 갚는 대신 다음 세대에게 베풀어 선의를 이어가다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this phrase when talking about mentorship, community building, or knowledge sharing — it signals a long-term, generous mindset that resonates strongly in founder and investor communities.

context: Brian Chesky is reflecting on Silicon Valley's culture of mentorship and mutual support, noting that successful founders help the next generation of entrepreneurs — not as a transaction but as a tradition. He personally finds that teaching others also deepens his own learning.

sentence: But I have this beginner's mindset that the bigger I get, the more a beginner I tend to feel. It's a weird feeling.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 깊은 경험을 쌓고도 초심자처럼 열린 호기심과 겸손함으로 세상을 바라보는 태도. 선불교의 초심(初心)과 맞닿아 있다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this phrase when talking about staying curious, avoiding overconfidence, or approaching an unfamiliar market with fresh eyes — it echoes the Zen concept of 'shoshin' and is widely cited in leadership and product thinking.

context: Brian Chesky is sharing a paradox he experiences as a seasoned CEO: the more Airbnb grows and the more he learns, the more acutely aware he becomes of how much he still doesn't know. vocabulary.meaning: a habitual or established attitude of mind that shapes how you interpret and respond to situations

sentence: some of the most memorable things were the moments where Dropbox really felt like it was taking on a life of its own

keyPhrase.surface: taking on a life of its own
keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 만든 사람의 손을 떠나 스스로 자라고 퍼져나가다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this when a project, campaign, or community has grown far beyond its original scope and is now driven by its own momentum rather than deliberate effort.

context: Drew Houston is reflecting on the most memorable moments in Dropbox's early growth. He describes the feeling of watching the product spread organically — beyond what the team had deliberately engineered — as one of the most exciting experiences of building the company.

vocabulary.meaning: a proposed explanation or assumption made as a starting point for further investigation

keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this in strategic discussions to warn about a competitor or market force that seems manageable today but will slowly and inevitably constrain your options if left unaddressed.

vocabulary.meaning: reached the highest point or final result after a period of development

vocabulary.meaning: expected or predicted in advance

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 실행에만 치여 정작 방향이 맞는지 멈춰 생각할 틈도 없는 상태

vocabulary.meaning: a metaphor for relentless, exhausting work that produces little forward progress

sentence: Or as Mark Twain put it, "Put all your eggs in one basket, and watch that basket." And I was like, "All right.

sentence: most of the entrepreneurs that are my heroes had various periods of wandering in the desert. Those things instead of just being problems were probably the crucible that forged the people that they became.

vocabulary.meaning: a severe test or trial that forces a transformation in those who endure it

Being correct isn't enough — if the market already agrees with you, there's no asymmetric opportunity left. The insights that create enormous value are the ones that sound wrong to most experts at the time.

vocabulary.meaning: to use something to maximum advantage

sentence: And so, they earn the secret by going down this rabbit hole of exploring something at the cutting edge for its own sake.

context: Mike Maples Jr. is describing how the most valuable startup insights are found. He argues that the founders who discover hidden truths about the future don't stumble upon them — they work their way to them by obsessively exploring an emerging technology or phenomenon for the love of it, long before there's a business reason to do so.

vocabulary.meaning: the most advanced and innovative stage of a field

sentence: If you're living in the future and you notice what's missing, your intuition about what to build is far more likely to be right.

keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this to push founders or product leaders to spend serious time using and building with emerging tools before deciding what to build next.

context: Mike Maples Jr. is explaining the mindset that produces the best product instincts. He argues that founders who operate at the frontier of a new technology develop an almost predictive sense of what the world will need — not from market research, but from direct lived experience in that future state.

vocabulary.meaning: the ability to understand something instinctively, without the need for conscious reasoning

sentence: So McCracken had a term for it that I've always liked lighthouse customers. And so the lighthouse has this light that shines out and obscures the fog.

sentence: And so I like to say, "You want to force a choice and not a comparison." If everybody's selling apples, I can't be a 10 times better Apple.

keyPhrase.surface: force a choice and not a comparison

context: Mike Maples Jr. is explaining how winning startups frame their market positioning. context: Mike Maples Jr. is reflecting on authenticity in leadership and storytelling, quoting journalist Robin Roberts. The idea is that the painful or chaotic chapters of your life aren't things to hide — they are the very experiences that give your message resonance and make others trust you.

sentence: Now it's worth double clicking on the product manager role because fundamentally, the designer and the engineering role are not hugely different.

context: Marty Cagan is explaining why the product manager role deserves special scrutiny when comparing it to other roles on a product team. vocabulary.meaning: at the most basic or essential level

keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this phrase to describe the challenge of aligning many independent-minded people around a shared goal — especially useful when discussing cross-functional projects or large organizations.

context: Marty Cagan is critiquing the limited scope of a product manager's role on a feature team, where the PM's primary job is to wrangle stakeholders and keep work moving rather than drive product strategy. He contrasts this with the more empowered PM role he advocates for.

vocabulary.meaning: more difficult or complex than it might appear at first

sentence: So you need to learn the techniques. That's the tools of the trade for a product manager.

vocabulary.meaning: a foundational rule or belief that guides decisions and behavior in an organization

sentence: And they think that the job is, "Yo, our job is to crank out features." And a lot of them don't even think twice because that's all they've ever known.

sentence: even props to Spotify, they've been able to hold their own against Apple and Amazon for so many years.

sentence: Now, let's work backwards from there and ask ourselves what went wrong, what could have contributed to this utter failure.
